frameworks.name: Fix-Then-Scale Sequencing
frameworks.definition: Optimize unit economics and margins in existing locations before opening new ones; expansion funded by proven profitability, not ambition.

frameworks.name: Sales Motion to Membership to Talent Ladder
frameworks.definition: Three-stage repair sequence for stalled multi-location service businesses: (1) fix sales motion, (2) install membership model to maximize revenue per technician, (3) raise pay and build career paths to retain talent — in that order.

claims.text: Alex Hormozi asserts that a med spa business reporting 15% net profit margin is too low to fund multi-location expansion safely, and that the priority must be restoring existing locations to 35–40% net margin before opening a fourth location.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
conditions: Multi-location service business (med spa)
Current net margin ~15%
Founder considering opening additional location
claims.mechanism: Expanding at sub-20% margins compounds operational debt across locations without generating sufficient reinvestment capital to fix the underlying unit economics.

numbers.axis: net profit margin target for med spa before expansion

claims.text: Alex Hormozi describes a consistent multi-location degradation pattern: the first location is founder-driven and highly profitable; the second location splits founder attention and earns lower margins; the third location is smaller by design and more efficient but has lower top-line revenue — resulting in roughly the same aggregate net profit as the first location alone, despite twice the liability.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
conditions: Founder-operated service business
Sequential multi-location expansion without talent backfill
claims.mechanism: Splitting founder attention across locations reduces the quality of founder-driven delivery and sales at each location, causing margins to fall roughly in proportion to the reduction in founder presence; the net result is higher total liability for nearly unchanged total profit.
bounded_by: Does not apply if the founder has already hired a replacement operator before opening the second location

claims.text: Alex Hormozi argues that when a med spa grows on organic and SEO-driven demand rather than paid acquisition (PPC, paid social), this reflects a favorable supply-demand market condition rather than a scalable business practice, and that owners who learn paid acquisition will outcompete those who do not.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
conditions: Med spa in a high-demand market (e.g., Austin, TX)
claims.mechanism: Organic demand disguises the absence of a repeatable acquisition system; when the market softens or a competitor invests in paid channels, organics-only operators have no paid playbook to defend revenue.
bounded_by: Does not apply to markets where supply-demand is not favorable; in those markets the absence of paid acquisition is already visible as a revenue problem

claims.text: Alex Hormozi contends that installing a universal membership enrollment motion — where every patient leaves with a membership — is the first operational lever for a stalled multi-location med spa because it simultaneously increases revenue retention, optimizes scheduling density, and raises revenue per square foot.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
conditions: Multi-location med spa with underutilized capacity
No current universal membership enrollment process
claims.mechanism: Recurring membership commits customers to a future visit schedule, allowing the practice to fill technician calendars in advance; higher scheduling density raises sessions per hour per technician, which directly lifts revenue per square foot without adding headcount.

claims.text: Alex Hormozi asserts that in med spas and any one-on-one personal-service business (tattoo, injections, personal training), top providers will leave and take a significant portion of the client base with them unless the business structures loyalty to the brand rather than to the individual provider.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
conditions: One-on-one personal-service business
High-rapport provider category (injectors, trainers, stylists)
claims.mechanism: Customers develop personal trust with the individual provider through repeated one-on-one sessions; when the provider departs, the customer's loyalty follows the individual because the relationship was never anchored to the brand.
bounded_by: Does not apply to businesses where service is standardized and interchangeable (e.g., commodity haircuts, urgent care)

claims.text: Alex Hormozi argues that the correct sequencing to reduce provider-to-brand loyalty risk in a med spa is: (1) fix the sales and membership model to raise margins, (2) use higher margins to increase provider compensation, (3) build a formal career path for injectors — in that order, because raising pay without margin headroom is not sustainable.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
conditions: Med spa with thin margins (~15%)
Key providers at risk of leaving with clients
claims.mechanism: Thin margins force a choice between paying providers market rate and staying solvent; only after the membership model raises revenue per technician does the business have the margin to fund both higher compensation and a career path simultaneously.

claims.text: Alex Hormozi states that a med spa's founder-driven revenue concentration is a critical fragility: the business described had the primary injector (Brooke) generating 60% of revenue at peak, and reducing that share to 23% required deliberate talent backfilling — suggesting that founder revenue concentration above roughly 30% is the threshold at which the business cannot safely expand.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
conditions: Med spa where primary founder is also the lead service provider

numbers.axis: founder revenue concentration in a med spa

bounded_by: Hormozi does not state an explicit safe threshold; the 30% figure is inferred from the narrative context, not stated verbatim

claims.text: Alex Hormozi recommends phantom stock tied to a vesting schedule as a retention mechanism for location-level providers and managers in multi-location service businesses, because standard equity grants transfer too much control and cash bonuses do not create long-term lock-in.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
conditions: Multi-location service business
Key providers or location GMs at risk of departure
claims.mechanism: Phantom stock creates economic exposure to business value appreciation without transferring voting control or cap-table dilution; vesting schedule creates a forward-looking financial incentive that increases cost-of-leaving over time.

claims.text: Alex Hormozi frames the correct repair sequence for a stalled multi-location med spa as: fix sales motion and membership model first (to raise revenue per technician), then use higher margins to increase provider compensation and build a career path, then step the founder out — because stepping out before margins are fixed produces a business that generates low income without the founder and is not replicable.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
conditions: Founder-operated multi-location med spa
Current net margin ~15%
Founder is primary or former primary revenue driver
claims.mechanism: Fixing the sales motion raises revenue per visit and scheduling density; higher margins create budget to raise provider pay and build career paths; only then does removing the founder not cause a revenue collapse, because the model runs on systems and staff rather than founder presence.

claims.text: Alex Hormozi asserts that the entrepreneurial instinct to pursue the next interesting idea (which he frames as shiny object syndrome) is a real, named constraint on business growth in founder-led companies, and that it is the job of the business to serve customers rather than to satisfy the founder's entrepreneurial curiosity.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
conditions: Founder-led business with more than one decision-maker (e.g., couple co-founders)
claims.mechanism: Entrepreneurial curiosity pulls attention toward unproven opportunities; the profitable base model suffers from reduced operational attention while management bandwidth is consumed by evaluating or launching new lines.

claims.text: Alex Hormozi states that a med spa owner who understands how to replicate a profitable location model has a capital-constrained growth problem, not a model-constrained problem, and that proving out the next location's model and filling it up is the key prerequisite before capital deployment.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
conditions: Med spa owner with 3 existing locations
Proven model on first location with strong ROI metrics
bounded_by: Does not apply if the existing locations have not yet proven profitability — in that case the constraint is model, not capital

examples.text: The med spa discussed (based in Austin, TX, three locations at $6.7M revenue) illustrates the multi-location margin degradation pattern: the first location performed well with Brooke (the primary injector and co-founder) driving the majority of revenue. When they opened a second downtown location, founder attention split and margins fell. The third location was deliberately smaller (1,200–1,500 sq ft) and more tightly scoped, producing better per-square-foot economics but lower top-line revenue. The combined result: roughly 15% net margin across all three — approximately the same net profit as the first location alone would have generated, despite triple the square footage and operating complexity.

The Austin med spa described its primary injector (Brooke) as a top-100 injector in the country whose personal reputation initially drove 60% of business revenue. After deliberate talent backfilling — hiring additional injectors to absorb demand — her share fell to 23%. The business used a phantom stock program tied to location-level vesting to retain the newly hired providers, rather than offering direct equity or pure cash bonuses.

The Austin med spa was shadow-banned on Instagram after a peptide post and had relied heavily on SEO and organic discovery for patient acquisition. Alex Hormozi pointed out that this reflected the favorable Austin supply-demand dynamic for med spas rather than a scalable acquisition capability, and that the owner should build paid acquisition skills to avoid competitive vulnerability when market conditions normalize.

practices.text: Build a universal membership enrollment motion so that every patient leaving a med spa visit is presented with a membership option; aim for a very high percentage of patients converting to membership to lock in scheduling predictability and recurring revenue.
Structure injector or provider career paths explicitly (levels, compensation milestones, leadership tracks) to increase loyalty to the brand rather than to the individual practitioner.
Pilot paid acquisition (PPC, paid social) before relying on organic-only growth in high-demand markets; organic demand is market-dependent and not a replicable system.
Pivot away from large-footprint wellness center concepts toward smaller, higher-ROI, tightly scoped locations (1,200–1,500 sq ft) focused on the highest-margin services, then replicate the model.
